subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Doctorate (PhD) in Mathematics from a leading research university.
Specialized in number theory and arithmetic geometry during doctoral studies.
Completed undergraduate studies in mathematics with honors.
Attended multiple advanced summer schools and workshops in algebraic geometry and related fields.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Current job title: Research Mathematician.
Role focuses on formalization of advanced mathematical theories using theorem provers.
Leads efforts in auto-formalization and automated theorem proving.
Responsible for designing formalization frameworks and coding in Lean.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the academic research and technology sector.
Operates at the intersection of pure mathematics and computer science.
Contributes to the growing field of AI-driven mathematical formalization.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Affiliated with a small research group or institute, typically under 50 members.
Team structure is highly collaborative and project-oriented.
Environment emphasizes innovation, research autonomy, and interdisciplinary work.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Operates in the mathematical research and AI-assisted proof technology segment.
Projects support both academic progress and development of automated math tools.
Focuses on open-source and academic contributions, not commercial software.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 6–10 years of experience in advanced mathematics roles post-PhD.
Transitioned to formalization and AI-related mathematics work 3–4 years ago.
Spent initial years after PhD focused on pure research in number theory.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Mid-career researcher with significant domain expertise.
Often takes lead or principal responsibilities on formalization projects.
Supervises or mentors junior researchers and PhD students in formal methods.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Designs and codes formalizations of mathematical theorems using Lean.
Decomposes large mathematical projects into smaller formalization goals.
Prepares and curates datasets for LLM training in auto-formalization.
Develops and maintains auto-theorem proving pipelines.
Regularly searches for relevant theorems and proof strategies within Lean’s libraries.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 60% of time on Lean coding, including formalization and proof writing.
Allocates 25% of work hours to LLM training, data preparation, and pipeline development.
Devotes around 10% of time to literature review and updating mathematical proofs.
The remaining 5% is spent on team meetings, documentation, and mentoring.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Works with a high degree of autonomy, independently setting research directions.
Makes key decisions regarding project decomposition and proof strategies.
Has authority to select tools, frameworks, and approaches for formalization.
Consults with peers for collaborative input but leads own subprojects.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Translating mathematical definitions into Lean syntax is straightforward.
Identifying and formalizing basic lemmas from existing literature feels routine.
Setting up the structure for new formalization projects comes naturally.
Using LeanSearch to find known theorems is an efficient and familiar process.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Filling in detailed Lean proofs for complex theorems is often challenging.
Generalizing and abstracting definitions for broader applicability requires deep insight.
Designing new formalization frameworks from scratch can be intellectually demanding.
Handling edge cases and gaps in existing mathematical literature is complex.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Searching theorems and proof patterns in large Lean libraries is repetitive.
Preparing training data for LLMs involves routine data cleaning and formatting.
Filling in boilerplate code for standard mathematical structures is time-consuming.
Validating and running auto-formalization pipelines could be further automated.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Designing innovative formalization frameworks is highly engaging.
Solving previously unformalized mathematical problems is rewarding.
Rewriting or debugging minor Lean code issues can feel tedious.
Manual data preparation for LLM training is less enjoyable.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Difficulty in finding the most relevant theorems within large libraries slows progress.
Existing AI tools sometimes generate incomplete or incorrect formal proofs.
Interoperability between Lean and data processing tools is limited.
Repetitive manual coding tasks consume significant time.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Enjoyment is highest when tackling novel or intellectually challenging problems.
Skill level boosts productivity, particularly in Lean coding and mathematical abstraction.
Productivity drops during repetitive or low-skill tasks such as manual data preparation.
A direct link is observed between deep engagement and both learning and output quality.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Enthusiastic about experimenting with new theorem provers and AI tools.
Views technological advancement as critical to advancing mathematical research.
Actively seeks out updates and new features in Lean and related ecosystems.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new coding environments, particularly in mathematics-focused tools.
Comfortable integrating new Python-based ML frameworks into workflow.
Able to self-learn and troubleshoot novel software with minimal guidance.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Regularly dedicates time to mastering updates in Lean and LLM workflows.
Participates in online workshops or webinars related to theorem proving and AI.
Prefers self-paced learning backed by documentation and real project application.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for efficiency gains and research breakthroughs.
Main barrier is limited time due to existing project commitments.
Relevance to core research or workflow is a key factor in prioritizing new tech adoption.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peer recommendations significantly influence tool experimentation.
Collaborative research projects often drive adoption of novel frameworks.
Management supports independent tool exploration and upskilling.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Lean interactive theorem prover as the main formalization tool.
Utilizes LeanSearch to locate relevant mathematical theorems rapidly.
Employs Python for developing and maintaining LLM training pipelines.
Leverages Deepseek for code generation and debugging.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on LaTeX for drafting mathematical documents and papers.
Uses Git for version control across code and formalization projects.
Employs VS Code as the primary code editor, especially with Lean extensions.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Highly proficient with Lean and its associated ecosystem.
Comfortable with Python for both data processing and machine learning tasks.
Familiar with industry-standard version control workflows (Git/GitHub).

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces friction integrating Lean outputs with Python-based ML pipelines.
Manual data transfer is sometimes required between formalization and AI systems.
Limited cross-compatibility between Lean and mainstream mathematical software.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands core LLM concepts such as tokenization and prompt engineering.
Comfortable with supervised learning, data preprocessing, and model evaluation.
Familiar with the basics of transformer architectures and their applications.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Regularly uses Deepseek for code generation and debugging.
Integrates LLM-based tools for auto-theorem proving within the workflow.
Explores open-source AI models compatible with Python for experimentation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies LLMs daily to automate parts of the formalization process.
Uses AI tools to search for relevant theorems and generate initial proof outlines.
Deploys AI for code completion and debugging during Lean and Python development.
Employs AI models for data augmentation and cleaning in training pipelines.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used Deepseek to auto-complete Lean code, saving hours on repetitive tasks.
Observed that LLM-generated proofs sometimes miss subtle mathematical nuances, requiring manual review.
AI-assisted theorem search has significantly accelerated project timelines.
Encountered limitations in LLMs when handling advanced, less-documented mathematical concepts.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Relies on peer collaboration and self-study for learning new AI tools.
Occasional internal workshops or seminars on AI in mathematics are available.
Most upskilling is self-driven, using open-source resources and documentation.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI, especially for complex proofs.
Uses independent AI suggestions for routine tasks and code generation.
Combines autonomous and interactive modes depending on problem complexity.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Treats AI tools primarily as intelligent assistants for coding and theorem search.
Occasionally leverages AI as a co-worker during intensive formalization projects.
Values AI's role in augmenting, rather than replacing, human expertise.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly receptive to integrating new AI workflows that demonstrably improve efficiency.
Willing to pilot experimental AI tools in live research projects.
Evaluates new AI-driven processes based on accuracy, transparency, and ease of use.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers command-based interfaces for precise, reproducible outputs in coding tasks.
Utilizes conversational AI interfaces for brainstorming or exploratory problem-solving.
Finds hybrid interfaces (e.g., chat with command options) most versatile.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Mathematical formalization heavily depends on deep, specialized domain expertise.
Tacit knowledge from years of research is critical for interpreting ambiguous statements.
Many proof steps require intuition not easily captured by current AI models.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated outputs for routine code and theorem search, but always reviews critical steps.
Skeptical of AI-generated proofs for novel or high-stakes theorems without manual verification.
Regularly checks LLM outputs against established mathematical literature.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive or boilerplate tasks, freeing time for creative problem-solving.
Sees human oversight as essential for high-level abstraction and quality control.
Envisions a workflow where AI accelerates groundwork and humans focus on innovation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for well-defined, low-risk coding and data preparation tasks.
Finds automation threatening if it compromises proof correctness or research quality.
Supports automation so long as manual override and review remain possible.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Views AI as enhancing the importance of abstraction and domain expertise.
Routine technical skills (e.g., boilerplate coding) may diminish in value.
Problem decomposition and creative reasoning remain essential and are less impacted.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing expertise in LLM training and prompt engineering.
Gaining proficiency in data curation and pipeline design for auto-formalization.
Expanding responsibilities to include AI evaluation and system improvement.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing core research and creative tasks.
Recognizes some risk to routine coding and data management roles.
Believes domain expertise and interpretation will remain human-centric for the foreseeable future.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always manually reviews AI-generated formal proofs before acceptance.
Implements checkpoints in auto-formalization pipelines for human verification.
Prefers tools that allow transparent auditing and rollback of AI decisions.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team policies encourage experimentation with new AI tools but require human oversight.
There is growing emphasis on training and skills development for AI integration.
Formal review processes for AI outputs are becoming more standardized.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will significantly accelerate mathematical research and discovery.
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to advanced mathematical tools.
Cautious about over-reliance on opaque AI systems in critical domains.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Advocates for transparency and reproducibility in AI-driven proofs.
Supports ethical guidelines to prevent misuse of automated theorem proving.
Wary of AI errors leading to propagation of incorrect mathematical results.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires an AI capable of auto-formalizing human-written proofs end-to-end.
Wishes for smarter AI theorem search and suggestion systems within Lean.
Would benefit from AI that can debug and explain formalization failures interactively.
Seeks integration of AI-assisted design pattern recognition for mathematical frameworks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects a greater focus on high-level abstraction and creative proof design.
Foresees routine formalization tasks becoming largely automated.
Anticipates new hybrid roles blending mathematics, AI, and software engineering.
Believes collaboration between mathematicians and AI/ML experts will intensify.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Invest in developing and maintaining custom AI tools for formal mathematics.
Establish robust review and verification protocols for AI-generated results.
Provide structured training and documentation for AI-driven workflows.
Foster interdisciplinary collaboration between mathematicians, AI researchers, and engineers.
